note:
γ batch input. 100 clusters.

For each cluster, output a verdict in strict JSON:
  {"cluster_hash": "<hash>", "word": "<word>", "decision": "merge|split|unsure", "confidence": 0.0-1.0, "reasoning": "1 sentence", "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100, "merged_text": "..." | null}

Save as: data/simplify_diff/gamma_batch_verdicts.json

GAMMA_SYSTEM_PROMPT:
You are an English vocabulary teacher reviewing 2-3 senses of a single word for an IELTS learner's flashcard.

YOUR RULE (the user's "80-90% rule"):
Merge the senses ONLY IF a single definition lets the learner understand AT LEAST 80-90% of all examples across the senses. Otherwise SPLIT.

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict JSON, no prose):
{
  "decision": "merge" | "split" | "unsure",
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0,
  "reasoning": "1 sentence",
  "examples_substitutable_pct": 0-100,
  "merged_text": "single definition" | null
}

DECISION RULES:
- merge:    1 def covers >=80% examples, learner doesn't need separate cards
- split:    senses are too distinct, need 2+ cards
- unsure:   borderline 70-80%, default to SPLIT in production (conservative)

NEVER merge senses that are clearly different POS, register, or context (e.g. "go" verb vs "go" noun).

senses.text: involving large numbers of people who work together to do something in a way that has been carefully planned
examples: an organized body of workers
an organized system of childcare
He was part of an organized criminal gang.
39% of male respondents stated that they regularly participated in organized sport compared with 17% of female respondents.

senses.text: arranged or planned well or in the way mentioned
examples: a carefully organized campaign
a well-organized office
a badly organized event
a loosely organized political alliance

senses.text: able to plan your work, life, etc. well and in an efficient way
examples: a very organized person
Isn't it time you started to get organized?

senses.text: to get well again after being ill, hurt, etc.
examples: He's still recovering from his operation.
She spent many weeks in hospital recovering from her injuries.
He has fully recovered from the shoulder surgery.
She remains in serious condition, but is expected to recover.
The victim is recovering well at home.

senses.text: to return to a normal state after an unpleasant or unusual experience or a period of difficulty
examples: It can take many years to recover from the death of a loved one.
The economy is at last beginning to recover.

senses.text: a person who supports a political party, an idea, etc.
examples: a strong/loyal/staunch/ardent supporter
Labour supporters
The magazine is free to members and supporters.
About a hundred of his supporters rallied to demand justice.

senses.text: a person who supports a particular sports team
examples: I'm an Arsenal supporter.

senses.text: a person whose business is buying and selling a particular product
examples: an art/antique dealer
He's a dealer in second-hand cars.

senses.text: a person who sells illegal drugs
examples: a crack dealer

senses.text: having the colour of blood or fire
examples: bright/light/dark red lipstick
The lights (= traffic lights) changed to red before I could get across.
a bunch of red roses
a red dress/shirt
the vivid red colour of the soldiers' uniforms

senses.text: red-brown in colour
examples: her flaming red hair
a red squirrel
a red-haired girl
Her hair was long and dark red.
She dyes her hair red.

senses.text: a system of government in which the people of a country can vote to elect their representatives
examples: parliamentary democracy
the principles of democracy

senses.text: a country that has this system of government
examples: Western democracies
I thought we were supposed to be living in a democracy.
The country is a multiparty democracy.

senses.text: a sheet of strong cloth which the wind blows against to make a boat or ship travel through the water
examples: As the boat moved down the river the wind began to fill the sails.
a ship under sail (= using sails)
in the days of sail (= when ships all used sails)
She moved away like a ship in full sail (= with all its sails spread out).
The vessel can be propelled by oars or sail (= sails).

senses.text: a trip in a boat or ship
examples: We went for a sail.
a two-hour sail across the bay
He took us for a sail up the river.

senses.text: a glass or plastic container, usually round with straight sides and a narrow neck, used especially for storing liquids
examples: a wine/beer/milk/water bottle
Put the top back on the bottle.
a plastic/glass bottle
To open the bottle, you twist and pull out the stopper.
He threw his message in a bottle into the North Sea.

senses.text: the amount contained in a bottle
examples: We drank a whole bottle of wine.
She handed me a bottle of water.

senses.text: to give something to somebody in exchange for money
examples: I recently sold my bike.
His parents went bankrupt and the family had to sell their home.
The board agreed to sell the company.
The shares were sold in the open market.
We offered them a good price but they wouldn't sell.

senses.text: to offer something for people to buy
examples: Most supermarkets sell a range of organic products.
Do you sell stamps?
to sell insurance
His books are sold worldwide.
She decided to sell her work in order to support her family.

senses.text: to experience something, especially something unpleasant or difficult, while you are trying to do something else
examples: We encountered a number of difficulties in the first week.
The report details the problems encountered by families in this situation.
I had never encountered such resistance before.

senses.text: to meet somebody, or discover or experience something, especially somebody/something new, unusual or unexpected
examples: She was the most remarkable woman he had ever encountered.
Plant scientists first encountered the phenomenon in the 1930s.

senses.text: a very short period of time
examples: Could you wait a moment, please?
One moment, please (= Please wait a short time).
He thought for a moment before replying.
For one brief moment I thought, ‘I can handle this.’
I'll be back in a moment.

senses.text: an exact point in time
examples: We're busy at the moment (= now).
At that very moment, the phone rang.
You need to be aware of what you are doing at any given moment.
I agreed in a moment of weakness.
From that moment on, she never felt really well again.

senses.text: a very unpleasant or frightening experience
examples: an account of the horrors inflicted on the detainees
The UN has been slow to condemn the horrors being perpetrated by the regime.
the horrors of war

senses.text: the very unpleasant nature of something, especially when it shocks or frightens you
examples: The full horror of the accident was beginning to become clear.
In his dreams he relives the horror of the attack.
Our report will reveal the true horror of conditions in the camps.
The horror of the situation overwhelmed him completely.

senses.text: a hollow place or structure that a bird makes or chooses for laying its eggs in and sheltering its young
examples: sparrows building a nest of twigs and dry grass

senses.text: a place where insects or other small creatures live and produce their young
examples: a wasps’ nest
a nest of mice
The snake will attack if disturbed inside its nest.

senses.text: to move people from a place of danger to a safer place
examples: Police evacuated nearby buildings.
Children were evacuated from London to escape the bombing.
Families were evacuated to safer parts of the city.

senses.text: to move out of a place because of danger, and leave the place empty
examples: Employees were urged to evacuate their offices immediately.
Locals were told to evacuate.

senses.text: a shape like a curve or circle made by a line curving right round
examples: The road went in a huge loop around the lake.
Duclair is a small town on a loop of the River Seine.

senses.text: a piece of rope, wire, etc. in the shape of a curve or circle
examples: He tied a loop of rope around his arm.
Make a loop in the string.
a belt loop (= on trousers, etc. for holding a belt in place)

senses.text: the state of having a healthy mind
examples: His behaviour was so strange that I began to question his sanity.
to keep/preserve your sanity

senses.text: the state of being sensible and reasonable
examples: After a series of road accidents the police pleaded for sanity among drivers.

senses.text: who or what somebody/something is
examples: The police are trying to discover the identity of the killer.
to establish/reveal/confirm the identity of somebody/something
Their names have been changed for the purposes of this article to protect their identities.
Their identities were kept secret.
Do you have any proof of identity?

senses.text: the characteristics, feelings or beliefs that make people different from others
examples: national/cultural/personal identity
ethnic/racial identity
The region's sense of identity remained strong.
a plan to strengthen the corporate identity of the company
Scotland has never lost its identity as a separate nation.

senses.text: a place where something happens or exists; the position of something
examples: a honeymoon in a secret location
Mobile phones can determine our exact location and relay it to the police in an emergency.
The harsh climate and remote location made mining even more difficult.
the country's strategic geographical/geographic location

senses.text: a place outside a film studio where scenes of a film are made
examples: The village was used as the location for a popular TV series.
The movie was shot entirely on location in Italy.
the exotic locations they filmed in
He went to the restaurant to scout a location for his next film.

senses.text: the act of investing money in something
examples: foreign/private investment
capital/business/property investment
to attract/encourage investment
This country needs investment in education.
This initiative involves considerable long-term investment by the university.

senses.text: the money that you invest, or the thing that you invest in
examples: a minimum investment of $10 000
a substantial/significant/major investment
Our investments are not doing well.
We bought the house as an investment (= to make money).
The area is trying to attract £11 million of private investment to stimulate the local economy.

senses.text: a thing that is worth buying because it will be useful or helpful
examples: A microwave is a good investment.

senses.text: making a lot of noise
examples: noisy children/traffic/crowds
He was kept awake by noisy neighbours.
a noisy protest (= when people shout)
The kids were even noisier than the dogs.
The engine is very noisy at high speed.

senses.text: full of noise
examples: a noisy classroom/office
People find it difficult to concentrate in noisy environments such as open-plan offices.
The streets were very noisy throughout the night.
The party was getting a bit noisy.
It was so noisy I couldn't hear myself speak.

senses.text: having a lot of money or property
examples: She's one of the richest women in the world.
to get/grow/become rich
Nobody gets rich from writing nowadays.
She longed to be rich and famous.
He was born in a rich and powerful family.

senses.text: producing a lot of wealth so that many of its people can live at a high standard
examples: the richest countries/economies/nations

senses.text: the state of being forced to work as a slave
examples: to be sold into slavery

senses.text: the practice of owning and forcing people to work as slaves
examples: the abolition of slavery
The Thirteenth Amendment abolished slavery in 1865.

senses.text: an official claim made by the police that somebody has committed a crime
examples: criminal charges
a murder/an assault charge
a charge of theft/rape/attempted murder
He will be sent back to England to face a charge of (= to be on trial for) armed robbery.
Both men deny the charges.

senses.text: a statement accusing somebody of doing something wrong or bad
examples: She rejected the charge that the story was untrue.
Be careful you don't leave yourself open to charges of political bias.

senses.text: showing that you really mean the opposite of what you are saying; expressing irony
examples: an ironic comment

senses.text: strange or funny because it is very different from what you expect
examples: It's ironic that she became a teacher—she used to hate school.

senses.text: a statue, stone, etc. that is built in order to remind people of an important past event or of a famous person who has died
examples: a war memorial (= in memory of soldiers who died in a war)
a memorial to victims of the Holocaust

senses.text: a thing that will continue to remind people of somebody/something
examples: The painting will be a lasting memorial to a remarkable woman.
a memorial of my husband

senses.text: having the colour of earth or coffee
examples: brown eyes/hair
brown bread/sugar/rice
dark brown shoe polish
a package wrapped in brown paper
Soon the yellow leaves will turn brown.

senses.text: having skin that is naturally brown or has been made brown by the sun
examples: I don't go brown very easily.
After the summer in Spain, the children were brown as berries.
He looked very brown after the cruise.

senses.text: an act or event that does not follow the laws of nature and is believed to be caused by God
examples: the miracle of rising from the grave

senses.text: a lucky thing that happens that you did not expect or think was possible
examples: an economic miracle
a miracle cure/drug
It's a miracle (that) nobody was killed in the crash.
It would take a miracle to make this business profitable.

senses.text: to pay no attention to something
examples: He ignored the 30 mph speed limit sign and accelerated down the road.
I made a suggestion but they chose to ignore it.
We cannot afford to ignore their advice.
to ignore a warning/a problem/an issue
She deliberately ignored my question and changed the subject.

senses.text: to pretend that you have not seen somebody or that somebody is not there
examples: She ignored him and carried on with her work.
If he tries to start an argument, just ignore him.

senses.text: that has existed for a long time and is therefore difficult to change
examples: ingrained prejudices
The belief that we should do our duty is deeply ingrained in most of us.

senses.text: under the surface of something and therefore difficult to get rid of
easily broken or damaged
examples: fragile china/glass/bones
Be careful not to drop it; it’s very fragile.

senses.text: weak and uncertain; easy to destroy or harm
examples: a fragile alliance/ceasefire/relationship
The economy remains extremely fragile.
In her job she was used to dealing with actors’ fragile egos.
fragile habitats threatened by pollution

senses.text: not strong and likely to become ill
examples: Her father is now 86 and in fragile health.
I'm feeling a bit fragile after last night (= not well, perhaps because of drinking too much alcohol).

senses.text: to say that something is not true
examples: to deny an allegation/a charge/an accusation
He has denied any involvement in the incident.
The company strongly denies wrongdoing.
to vehemently/strenuously/categorically deny something
A spokesperson refused to either confirm or deny the reports.

senses.text: to refuse to admit or accept something
examples: She denied all knowledge of the incident.
The department denies responsibility for what occurred.
an anti-environmentalist campaign group that denies climate change

senses.text: to happen; to start to exist
examples: An opportunity arose to work in the United States.
Questions naturally arose as to who was responsible.
A serious problem can arise if the heart stops pumping effectively.
We only use the car when the need arises (= when it is necessary).
This issue arose as an unintended consequence of the Act.

senses.text: to happen as a result of a particular situation
examples: injuries arising out of a road accident
Emotional or mental problems can arise from a physical cause.
Are there any matters arising from the minutes of the last meeting?

senses.text: a set of ideas that an economic or political system is based on
examples: Marxist/capitalist ideology

senses.text: a set of beliefs, especially one held by a particular group, that influences the way people behave
examples: the ideology of gender roles
alternative ideologies

senses.text: the possibility of something happening that will injure, harm or kill somebody, or damage or destroy something
examples: Danger! Keep Out!
Firefighters face danger every day.
Children's lives are in danger every time they cross this road.
in grave/mortal danger
They found themselves in grave danger.

senses.text: the possibility of something bad or unpleasant happening
examples: There is no danger of a bush fire now.
They reminded residents of the potential dangers of living so near an active volcano.
‘Nicky won't find out, will she?’ ‘Oh, no, there's no danger of that.’
The building is in danger of collapsing.
How many people are in danger of losing their jobs?

senses.text: a person or thing that may cause damage, or harm somebody
examples: There are many hidden dangers for small children in the home.
Smoking is a serious danger to health.
Police said the man was a danger to the public.

senses.text: a series of things that are done in order to achieve a particular result
examples: the consultation/planning process
Each time we have to go through the whole decision-making process again.
Repeat this process until the entire area is tiled.
I'm afraid getting things changed will be a slow process.
Getting divorced was a long and painful (= difficult) process for him.

senses.text: a series of things that happen, especially ones that result in natural changes
examples: It's a normal part of the learning process.
The drug has been found to significantly speed up the healing process.
Tiny crystals that form naturally in the cheese during the ageing process to give it a delicate crunchy texture.
Everything is in a process of change.

senses.text: a method of doing or making something, especially one that is used in industry
examples: manufacturing processes
She is involved in the production process from start to finish.
a new process for rustproofing car bodies

senses.text: to treat a person or situation as an opportunity to gain an advantage for yourself
examples: He exploited his father's name to get himself a job.
She realized that her youth and inexperience were being exploited.
The opposition parties will always exploit government problems to their own advantage.

senses.text: to treat somebody unfairly by making them work and not giving them much in return
examples: What is being done to stop employers from exploiting young people?
The workers are ruthlessly exploited by their employers.

senses.text: a metal container in which food is sold; the contents of one of these containers
examples: Open the tin and drain the tuna.
a tin of beans/soup
Next, add two tins of tomatoes.
a sardine tin

senses.text: a metal container with a lid (= cover), in the shape of a cylinder, in which paint, glue, etc. is sold and stored; the contents of one of these containers
examples: a tin of varnish
The bedroom needed three tins of paint (= in order to paint it).
a paint tin

senses.text: a metal container used for cooking food in
examples: a cake/loaf tin

senses.text: very bad weather with strong winds and rain, and often thunder and lightning
examples: fierce/heavy/severe/violent storms
Winter storms swept the coasts.
Her home was hit by two tropical storms.
A few minutes later the storm broke (= began).
I think we're in for a storm (= going to have one).

senses.text: very bad weather of the type mentioned
examples: She had to brave an ice storm to get to the interview.

senses.text: showing that you approve of somebody/something or that you share their views and are willing to support them
examples: He was speaking to a highly sympathetic audience.
to be sympathetic to the party’s aims
Russian newspapers are largely sympathetic to the president.

senses.text: easy to like
examples: a sympathetic character in a novel
I don't find her a very sympathetic person.
The character's faults actually make him more sympathetic.

senses.text: a system that provides something that the public needs, organized by the government or a private company
examples: the ambulance/bus/postal service
The government aims to improve public services, especially education.
Essential services (= the supply of water, gas, electricity) will be maintained.
Councillors have called for better provision of services for young people.
The charity provides a vital service to the local community.

senses.text: an organization or a company that provides something for the public or does something for the government
examples: the prison service
the BBC World Service
the Foreign Service

senses.text: a business whose work involves doing something for customers but not producing goods; the work that such a business does
examples: financial services
the development of new goods and services
Smith's Catering Services (= a company) offers the best value.
We guarantee (an) excellent service.
We aim to provide a high-quality service.

senses.text: not very obvious or easy to notice
examples: subtle colours/flavours/smells, etc.
There are subtle differences between the two versions.
The fragrance is a subtle blend of jasmine and sandalwood.
She's been dropping subtle hints about what she'd like as a present.

senses.text: behaving or organized in a clever way, and using indirect methods, in order to achieve something
examples: I decided to try a more subtle approach.
a subtle plan
a subtle use of lighting in the play

senses.text: the act or process of putting people or things into a group or class (= of classifying them)
examples: a style of music that defies classification (= is like no other)

senses.text: a group, class, division, etc. into which somebody or something is put
examples: a document with the security classification ‘confidential’
The material was put into the highest security classification.
These messages could be said to fall under the classification of ‘abuse’.
Electric bikes are in the same classification as mopeds.
The classification ‘science’ covers a great many different subjects.

senses.text: a public official who charges somebody officially with a crime and prosecutes them in court
examples: the public/state prosecutor

senses.text: a lawyer who leads the case against a defendant in court
examples: The prosecutor rose to give the opening address.

senses.text: the treatment of a physical problem or an illness
examples: He is receiving therapy for cancer.
Most leukaemia patients undergo some sort of drug therapy (= treatment using drugs).
alternative/complementary therapies (= treatments that do not use traditional drugs)
new cancer therapies
They recommend exercise therapy for patients with chronic back pain.

senses.text: the treatment of problems with somebody's mental health by talking with them
examples: She's in therapy.
I went into therapy because my doctor suggested it.
the use of hypnosis as a form of therapy
a therapy group/session

senses.text: drinks such as beer, wine, etc. that can make people drunk
examples: He never drinks alcohol.
alcohol abuse/use/dependence
the dangers of excessive alcohol consumption
Alcohol should only be consumed in moderation.
Licences to sell alcohol are granted by local councils.

senses.text: the clear liquid that is found in drinks such as beer, wine, etc. and is used in medicines, cleaning products, etc.
examples: Wine contains about 10% alcohol.
The food and drink industry has responded to the demand for low- and no-alcohol drinks.
levels of alcohol in the blood
She was arrested for driving under the influence of alcohol.
He pleaded guilty to driving with excess alcohol.

senses.text: the group of people who have gathered to watch or listen to something (a play, concert, somebody speaking, etc.)
examples: The audience was/were clapping for 10 minutes.
The debate was televised in front of a live audience.
an audience of 10 000
Audience members voted on their favorite presentations.
We encourage audience participation during our show.

senses.text: a number of people or a particular group of people who watch, read, or listen to the same thing
examples: TV/television/cinema/movie audiences
His book reached an even wider audience when it was made into a movie.
This book will appeal to a broad audience.
She has generated a massive online audience since uploading her first clip three years ago.
The target audience for this advertisement was mainly teenagers.

senses.text: a way of doing something, especially the usual or correct way
examples: emergency/safety/disciplinary procedures
Making a complaint is quite a simple procedure.
The school in this case did not follow the correct procedure.
to follow normal/standard/the proper procedure
To ensure high quality, all products go through rigorous testing procedures.

senses.text: a medical operation
examples: a routine surgical procedure
to perform/undergo a procedure

senses.text: to decide or say officially that something is not allowed
examples: This law bans the use of dangerous pesticides.
a list of banned substances
The use of these chemicals in homes was banned outright by the government in 2000.
a campaign to ban sugary drinks from schools

senses.text: to order somebody not to do something, go somewhere, etc., especially officially
examples: He was banned from the meeting.
She was banned from the road for ten months for drinking and driving.
She's been banned from leaving the country while the allegations are investigated.
He was banned from driving for six months.
The sprinter has been banned for life after failing a drugs test.

senses.text: the act of stopping a vehicle at a place and leaving it there for a period of time
examples: There is no parking here between 9 a.m. and 6 p.m.
I managed to find a parking space.
I got there early to grab a parking spot.
I pulled the car over into a parking bay.
a parking fine (= for parking illegally)

senses.text: a space or an area for leaving vehicles
examples: The hotel is centrally situated with ample free parking.
parking for 300 cars
There is off-street parking at the front of the house.
The town has free on-street parking.
The public did not vote for increased car parking charges.

senses.text: the process of keeping something in a particular place until it is needed; the space where things can be kept
examples: tables that fold flat for storage
There's a lot of storage space in the loft.
food storage facilities
We need more storage now.

senses.text: the process of keeping information, etc. on a computer; the way it is kept
examples: the storage and retrieval of information
data storage

senses.text: the process of paying to keep furniture, etc. in a special building until you want it
examples: When we moved we had to put our furniture in storage for a while.

senses.text: to put clothes, etc. into a bag in preparation for a trip away from home
examples: I haven't packed yet.
I have to start packing for my trip.
I haven't packed my suitcase yet.
I packed my bags and left.
Did you pack the camera?

senses.text: to put something into a container so that it can be stored, transported or sold
examples: The pottery was packed in boxes and shipped to the US.
I carefully packed up the gifts.
Ryan busied himself packing everything up into bags.
He found a part-time job packing eggs.

senses.text: feeling fear; frightened because you think that you might be hurt or suffer
examples: Don't be afraid.
It's all over. There's nothing to be afraid of now.
He had always been afraid of dogs.
They know I'm afraid of heights.
Are you afraid of spiders?

senses.text: worried about what might happen
examples: She was afraid of upsetting her parents.
He was afraid of making a fool of himself.
Don't be afraid to ask if you don't understand.
She certainly isn't afraid to speak her mind.
We were afraid (that) we were going to capsize the boat.

senses.text: worried or frightened that something unpleasant, dangerous, etc. will happen to a particular person or thing
examples: I'm not afraid for me, but for the baby.
They had already fired three people and he was afraid for his job.
I am very afraid for their safety.
I was afraid for my life.

senses.text: something that you need or want
examples: the basic requirements of life
a software solution to meet your requirements
All products can be customized to suit your specific requirements.
Our immediate requirement is extra staff.
These goods are surplus to requirements (= more than we need).

senses.text: something that you must have in order to do something else
examples: to meet/fulfil/satisfy the requirements
What is the minimum entry requirement for this course?
Banks now face stricter regulatory requirements.

senses.text: the fact of a large number of people or things arriving somewhere, especially people or things that are unpleasant
examples: the annual tourist invasion
Farmers are struggling to cope with an invasion of slugs.
Robberies and home invasions are grim facts of daily life.

senses.text: an act or a process that affects somebody/something in a way that is not welcome
examples: The singer described the photographs of her as an invasion of privacy.

senses.text: a person that you do not know
examples: There was a complete stranger sitting at my desk.
They got on well together although they were total strangers.
Why should a perfect (= complete) stranger ever help me out?
We've told our daughter not to speak to strangers.
She remained a stranger to me.

senses.text: a person who is in a place that they have not been in before
examples: Sorry, I don't know where the bank is. I'm a stranger here myself.
He must have been a stranger to the town.

senses.text: to become impossible to see
examples: With that, Matt promptly disappeared.
The mirror made the ceiling seem to disappear.
The plane disappeared behind a cloud.
Lisa watched until the train disappeared from view.
Lucy watched his tall figure disappear into the cafe.

senses.text: to stop existing
examples: Her nervousness quickly disappeared once she was on stage.
The problem won't just disappear.
Our countryside is disappearing at an alarming rate.
Wildlife is fast disappearing from our countryside.
These fish have virtually disappeared from their traditional waters.

senses.text: to be lost or impossible to find
examples: I can never find a pen in this house. They disappear as soon as I buy them.
Then one day he simply disappeared.
The plane mysteriously disappeared over the Indian Ocean.
The child disappeared from his home some time after four.

senses.text: having a strong effect on your body or mind
examples: a potent drug
a very potent alcoholic brew
a potent argument
That chilli sauce is pretty potent stuff.

senses.text: powerful
examples: a potent force

senses.text: a flat thin piece of any material, normally square or rectangular
examples: a sheet of glass/plastic/plywood/steel
sheet metal (= metal that has been made into thin sheets)

senses.text: a wide flat area of something, covering the surface of something else
examples: The road was covered with a sheet of ice.

senses.text: to be the right shape and size for somebody/something
examples: I tried the dress on but it didn't fit.
That jacket fits well.
My shoes fit perfectly.
a close-fitting dress
I can't find clothes to fit me.

senses.text: to be of the right size, type or number to go somewhere
examples: I'd like to have a desk in the room but it won't fit.
This device is small enough to fit in a pocket.
His arms fitted snugly around me.

senses.text: matters connected with getting or using power within a particular group or organization
examples: I don't want to get involved in office politics.
the internal politics of the legal profession
racial/gender/sexual politics (= connected with relationships of power between different groups in society)
the politics of identity/race/gender
the politics of fear/envy/division/hate (= when people are influenced by fear, envy, etc.)

senses.text: a person’s political views or beliefs
examples: His politics are extreme.

senses.text: things that are produced to be sold
examples: to produce/buy/sell goods
manufactured/imported goods
luxury goods
electrical/sporting goods
cheap/expensive goods

senses.text: possessions that can be moved
examples: He was accused of handling stolen goods.
In 1872 Ann loaded her five children and her household goods onto a wagon and travelled north.
The plastic bag contained all his worldly goods (= everything he owned).

senses.text: a district or an area of a town; the people who live there
examples: We grew up in the same neighbourhood.
a poor/quiet/residential neighbourhood
an old working-class neighbourhood
Manhattan is divided into distinct neighborhoods.
the neighbourhood police

senses.text: the area that you are in or the area near a particular place
examples: We searched the surrounding neighbourhood for the missing boy.
It was a car from outside the immediate neighbourhood.
Houses in the neighbourhood of Paris are extremely expensive.

senses.text: the act of going to different parts of a building, an area, etc. to make sure that there is no trouble or crime
examples: Security guards make regular patrols at night.
a police car on patrol

senses.text: a group of soldiers, vehicles, etc. that patrol an area
examples: a naval/police patrol
a patrol car/boat

senses.text: to tell somebody what you think they should do in a particular situation
examples: I would strongly advise against going out on your own.
They advised me against visiting the troubled south of the country.
The Foreign Office is advising Britons against all non-essential travel to the region.
They advised against the use of military force.
Her mother was away and couldn't advise her.

senses.text: to give somebody help and information on a subject that you know a lot about
examples: We employ an expert to advise on new technology.
The nurse will advise about getting out of bed, bathing, and diet.
She advises the government on environmental issues.
They can advise customers about safely using and storing pesticides.
The pharmacist will advise which medicines are safe to take.

senses.text: a small piece of a substance with many even sides, that is formed naturally when the substance becomes solid
examples: ice/salt crystals

senses.text: a clear mineral, such as quartz, used in making jewellery and attractive objects
examples: crystal earrings

senses.text: the star that shines in the sky during the day and gives the earth heat and light
examples: The sun was shining and birds were singing.
the sun’s rays
The sun rises highest in the sky during the summer.
the rising/setting sun
The sun was just setting.

senses.text: the light and heat from the sun
examples: the warmth of the afternoon sun
The sun was blazing hot.
This room gets the sun in the mornings.
We sat in the sun.
The harvested crop is dried in the hot Indian sun.

senses.text: a long narrow passage in a building, with doors that open into rooms on either side
examples: His room is along the corridor.

senses.text: a passage along the side of some railway trains, from which doors lead into separate compartments
the act of getting something, especially knowledge, a skill, etc.
examples: theories of child language acquisition

senses.text: something that somebody buys to add to what they already own, usually something valuable
examples: His latest acquisition is a racehorse.
The money will be spent on acquisitions for the university library.

senses.text: a company, piece of land, etc. bought by somebody, especially another company; the act of buying it
examples: They have made acquisitions in several EU countries.
the acquisition of shares by employees
The group has announced its first overseas acquisition: a successful software company.
The company has just made another acquisition.

senses.text: an arrangement to meet somebody at a particular time
examples: Call me next week and we'll try and make a date.
Next Friday? Fine—it's a date!

senses.text: a meeting that you have arranged with a boyfriend or girlfriend or with somebody who might become a boyfriend or girlfriend
examples: I've got a date with Lucy tomorrow night.
We agreed to go out on a date.
Do you think I should ask him out on a date?
Paul's not coming. He's got a hot date (= an exciting one).
She's out on a date with her new boyfriend.

senses.text: a person who fights against the government of their country
examples: rebel forces
Armed rebels advanced towards the capital.
Some parts of the country fell into rebel hands.

senses.text: a person who opposes somebody in authority over them within an organization, a political party, etc.
examples: A number of Tory rebels are planning to vote against the government.
The Education Secretary has made further concessions to the back-bench rebels.

senses.text: a person who does not like to obey rules or who does not follow generally accepted standards of behaviour, dress, etc.
examples: I've always been the rebel of the family.
He was a rebel in his teens but he’s a respectable citizen these days.

senses.text: the person who is in charge of a college or (in Scotland and Canada) a university
examples: Peter Brown, principal of St John’s College

senses.text: a teacher who is in charge of a school
examples: Principal Ray Smith
The regulations have been opposed by local high school principals.

senses.text: the amount by which money spent or owed is greater than money earned in a particular period of time
examples: a budget deficit
The trade balance has been in deficit for the past five years.

senses.text: the amount by which something, especially an amount of money, is too small or smaller than something else
examples: There's a deficit of $3 million in the total needed to complete the project.
The team has to come back from a 2–0 deficit in the first half.

senses.text: the amount of something that a person, a machine or an organization produces
examples: Manufacturing output has increased by 8 per cent.

senses.text: the information, results, etc. produced by a computer
examples: data output
an output device

senses.text: either of two equal parts into which something is or can be divided
examples: two and a half kilos (= 2½)
One and a half hours are allowed for the exam.
An hour and a half is allowed for the exam.
in the first half of the 20th century
The second half of the book is more exciting.

senses.text: either of two periods of time into which a sports game, concert, etc. is divided
examples: No goals were scored in the first half.
She played well in the second half of the match.
He made two superb saves in the first two minutes of the half.
We had a few chances to score in both halves.

senses.text: one of a series of things that you do in order to achieve something
examples: We are taking steps to prevent pollution.
It's a big step giving up your job and moving halfway across the world.
a necessary/an important/a positive step
This was a first step towards a united Europe.
This won't solve the problem but it's a step in the right direction.

senses.text: one of a series of things that somebody does or that happen, which forms part of a process
examples: Having completed the first stage, you can move on to step 2.
If you follow all the steps, nothing will go wrong.
I'd like to take this idea a step further.
This was a big step up (= to a better position) in his career.
I'll explain it to you step by step.

senses.text: wealth or property that is owned by a business or a person and can be invested or used to start a business
examples: share/investment/equity capital
He had various ideas on how to raise capital for the project.
a guide to investing capital in new markets
Our capital is all tied up in property.
capital investment (= money invested in a business)

senses.text: an amount of money that is invested or is used to start a business
examples: to set up a business with a starting capital of £100 000

senses.text: a vehicle (smaller than a ship) that travels on water, moved by oars, sails or a motor
examples: a rowing/sailing boat
a fishing boat
You can take a boat trip along the coast.
The country intends to build a special fleet of patrol boats.
a rescue/pleasure/passenger boat

senses.text: any ship
examples: ‘How are you going to France?’ ‘We're going by boat (= by ferry).’
The island is just a short boat ride from the mainland.

senses.text: to say officially that somebody/something has a particular character or name; to describe somebody/something in a particular way
examples: This area has been designated (as) a National Park.
The school has been designated a specialist science academy.
Several pupils were designated as having moderate or severe learning difficulties.
a designated nature reserve
designated seats for the elderly

senses.text: to choose or name somebody for a particular job or position
examples: The director is allowed to designate his/her successor.
Who has she designated (as) her deputy?
the man designated to succeed the president

senses.text: a thing that holds something and prevents it from falling
examples: The supports under the bridge were starting to bend.
When my father died, Jim was a real support.

senses.text: the act of holding something in position or preventing it from falling
examples: She held on to his arm for support.
After the operation he couldn't walk without support.
A firmer mattress gives your back an improved level of support.

senses.text: something you wear to hold an injured or weak part of your body in position
examples: a knee/back support

senses.text: exactly the one or ones referred to or mentioned; not different
examples: We have lived in the same house for twenty years.
Our children go to the same school as theirs.
She's still the same fun-loving person that I knew at college.
This one works in exactly the same way as the other.
They promise to meet again in the exact same place six months later.

senses.text: exactly like the one or ones referred to or mentioned
examples: I bought the same car as yours (= another car of that type).
She was wearing the same dress that I had on.
The same thing happened to me last week.
Most parents tend to raise their children in much the same way they were raised
Other countries don't suffer from the same kinds of health problems we do.

senses.text: to be present at an event
examples: We'd like as many people as possible to attend.
The meeting was attended by 90% of shareholders.
to attend a wedding/funeral/service/ceremony
Everyone is welcome to attend the free event.
All members are requested to attend this meeting.

senses.text: to go regularly to a place
examples: Our children attend the same school.
Students are expected to attend class regularly.
How many people attend church every Sunday?
Your dentist will ask you to attend for regular check-ups.
The child was failing to attend regularly at the school.

senses.text: not wet or sticky; without water or moisture
examples: Is my shirt dry yet?
Store onions in a cool dry place.
I'm afraid this cake has turned out very dry.
Some plants grow better in dry soil.
When the paint is completely dry, apply another coat.

senses.text: with very little rain
examples: weeks of hot, dry weather
the dry season
I hope it stays dry for our picnic.
There is every prospect of the weather remaining dry this week.
Rattlesnakes occur in the warmer, drier parts of North America.

senses.text: the way you are feeling at a particular time
examples: She's in a good mood today (= happy and friendly).
He's always in a bad mood (= unhappy, or angry and impatient).
to be in a foul/festive mood
Wait until he’s in a better mood before you ask him.
I'm just not in the mood for a party tonight.

senses.text: the way a group of people feel about something; the atmosphere in a place or among a group of people
examples: The mood of the meeting was distinctly pessimistic.
The movie captures the mood of the interwar years perfectly.
He threw in some jokes to lighten the mood.
But the mood of the country has changed.
Haunting music greatly adds to the mood of the film.

senses.text: a person who listens
examples: a good listener (= somebody who you can rely on to listen with attention or sympathy)
a sympathetic/willing listener
an attentive listener

senses.text: a person listening to a radio programme or podcast
examples: radio listeners
Regular listeners will know this is the Year of Czech Music.
Listeners to Radio 4 have plenty to look forward to this week.

senses.text: that can be understood in more than one way; having different meanings
examples: an ambiguous word/term/statement
Her account was deliberately ambiguous.

senses.text: not clearly stated or defined
examples: His role has always been ambiguous.

senses.text: obtained by using your feelings rather than by considering the facts
examples: He had an intuitive sense of what the reader wanted.

senses.text: able to understand something by using feelings rather than by considering the facts
examples: She was an intuitive person, quickly understanding what people wanted and needed.

senses.text: easy to understand and to use
known about by only a few people; kept hidden from others
examples: secret information/meetings/talks
They belong to an age-old secret society.
He tried to keep it secret from his family.
Details of the proposals remain secret.
a secret passage leading to the beach

senses.text: used to describe actions and behaviour that you do not tell other people about
examples: He's a secret drinker.
I didn't know you were a secret football fan.
her secret fears
Perhaps he has secret hopes that she'll go back to him.

senses.text: a business or an organization that provides a particular service especially when representing other businesses or organizations
examples: She works for an advertising agency.
He managed to find a job through an agency.

senses.text: a government department that provides a particular service
examples: law enforcement agencies
Some laboratories are operated by government agencies.

senses.text: an official order that limits trade, contact, etc. with a particular country, in order to make it do something, such as obeying international law
examples: Trade sanctions were imposed against any country that refused to sign the agreement.
The economic sanctions have been lifted.

senses.text: a course of action that can be used, if necessary, to make people obey a law or behave in a particular way
examples: The ultimate sanction will be the closure of the restaurant.
We now have an effective sanction against the killing of whales.

senses.text: always behaving in the same way, or having the same opinions, standards, etc.
examples: a consistent approach to the problem
a consistent pattern of behaviour
He has been Milan's most consistent player (= who plays well most often) this season.
She's not very consistent in the way she treats her children.
We must be consistent in applying the rules.

senses.text: happening in the same way and continuing for a period of time
examples: We have had enough of the party's consistent failure to come up with any new policies.
There has been a pattern of consistent growth in the economy.
Defence spending levels have remained consistent.
The Sunday paper failed to sell its target of 30 000 copies a week on a consistent basis.

senses.text: to change the form of something; to change in form
examples: The photochemical reactions transform the light into electrical impulses.
Materials are transformed from their original state into other forms.
I am convinced that the electric power industry is transforming.
The way we work, play and communicate every day has completely transformed from a decade ago.

senses.text: to completely change the appearance or character of something/somebody, especially so that it is better; to completely change in appearance or character
examples: A new colour scheme will transform your bedroom.
It was an event that would transform my life.
During the performance the artist transforms herself with the aid of make-up and a wig.
London's Docklands have been radically transformed over the last 20 years.
He seems to have been miraculously transformed into a first-class player.

senses.text: making goods or growing crops, especially in large quantities
examples: highly productive farming land
productive workers
The aim was to maximize the machine’s productive capacity.
The bush must be pruned to remain productive.

senses.text: doing or achieving a lot
examples: a productive meeting
My time spent in the library was very productive.

senses.text: if two or more substances or things mix or you mix them, they combine, usually in a way that means they cannot easily be separated
examples: Oil and water do not mix.
Oil does not mix with water.
Grief mixed with fear and rage as the people surveyed the ruins of their homes.
Mix all the ingredients together in a bowl.
If you mix blue and yellow, you get green.

senses.text: to prepare something by combining two or more different substances
examples: With this range of paints, you can mix your own colours.
Be sure to mix the solution properly .
Why don't you mix a cocktail for our guests?
Why don't you mix our guests a cocktail?

senses.text: a building or part of a hospital where people can go for special medical treatment or advice
examples: He was treated at the hospital's eye clinic.
The patient then returns to the outpatient clinic two weeks later.

senses.text: a period of time during which doctors give special medical treatment or advice
examples: The antenatal clinic is on Wednesdays.
Doctors held a special clinic to talk to worried parents after a child at the school died of meningitis.

senses.text: a private hospital or one that treats health problems of a particular kind
examples: He is being treated at the London clinic.
a rehabilitation clinic for people with alcoholism

senses.text: a building shared by a group of doctors who work together
an increase in an amount, a number or a level
examples: The industry is feeling the effects of recent price rises.
a tax rise
an interest rate rise
the rise and fall of daily temperatures
a rapid temperature rise

senses.text: an increase in the money you are paid for the work you do
examples: I'm going to ask for a rise.
He criticized the huge pay rises awarded to industry bosses.
Wage rises have been held at 1 per cent.
The union is demanding an across-the-board rise of 5 per cent.

senses.text: to make somebody feel ashamed
examples: His generosity shamed them all.
The people who did this all deserve to be publicly shamed.

senses.text: to make somebody feel that they have lost honour or respect
examples: You have shamed your family.

senses.text: the state of being kept in a place, especially a prison, and prevented from leaving
examples: They were sentenced to 12 months' detention in a young offender institution.
police powers of arrest and detention
allegations of torture and detention without trial
Prisoners have the right to challenge their detentions.
a detention camp

senses.text: the punishment of being kept at school for a time after other students have gone home
examples: They can’t give me (a) detention for this.

senses.text: an occasion when people eat food, especially breakfast, lunch or dinner
examples: Try not to eat between meals.
Lunch is his main meal of the day.
to go out for a meal (= to go to a restaurant to have a meal)
What time would you like your evening meal?

senses.text: the food that is eaten at a meal
examples: Enjoy your meal.
a three-course meal
They are learning to cook simple, healthy meals.

senses.text: very large in size or amount and seeming to have no end
examples: endless patience
endless opportunities for making money
The possibilities are endless.
an endless list of things to do
We don't have an endless supply of money, you know.

senses.text: continuing for a long time and seeming to have no end
examples: an endless round of parties and visits
The journey seemed endless.

senses.text: a young human who is not yet an adult
examples: a child of 3/a 3-year-old child
men, women and children
an organization that campaigns for the rights of the unborn child
The film is not suitable for young children.
I lived in London as a child.

senses.text: a son or daughter of any age
examples: They have three grown-up children.
All four of their adult children work in the family business.
They can't have children.
